items.rationale: His foundational research area; treats TFM design as a rich, solvable theory problem and celebrates resolving its core open questions.

citations.quote: the main open theory question from my original work on EIP-1559 has been resolved... no transaction fee mechanism can be DSIC, MMIC, and OCA-proof!
Major update to the paper... on transaction fee mechanism design in a post-MEV world... now with searchers... and tight welfare guarantees

items.rationale: Believes MEV fundamentally breaks incentive compatibility and that mitigation infrastructure is provably necessary — a frontier he actively researches.
items.age_note: Core thesis stated 2023-07; reinforced through 2024 TFM work.

citations.quote: And boy does MEV change things; for example, EIP-1559 is no longer incentive-compatible for both users and block producers
Lots of competing proposals... OFAs... encrypted mempools, MPC, TEEs, etc. But do we truly need any of these? Yes!

items.rationale: Largest body of his work; sees mapping the trade-offs across degrees of permissionlessness (longest-chain vs PBFT-style PoS) as fundamental and fascinating.

citations.quote: Should proof-of-stake blockchain protocols follow the longest-chain tradition of Bitcoin, the PBFT-style tradition... The trade-offs are pretty fascinating
New two-hour tutorial by @AndrewLewisPye on our "Permissionless Consensus" paper

items.rationale: Most recent enthusiasm; views practical SNARK progress as a gateway to making 'crazier' cryptographic primitives practical, citing record-fast zkVM built from theory.
items.age_note: Excitement persists from 2024-04 (Jolt/zkVM) to 2024-12, the most recent statements.

citations.quote: OH (from a famous theoretical computer scientist): "if SNARKs can be made practical, *anything* can be made practical"
It's been amazing watching 102 pages of hard theory get turned into the fastest zkVM in the world

items.rationale: Actively teaching and researching loss-versus-rebalancing and constant-function market makers as the analytic core of DeFi.

citations.quote: Videos from my LVR tutorial with @jason_of_cs @ciamac at EC '24 last summer are up!

items.rationale: Explores why Dutch auctions dominate Web3 and how on-chain auction design can disincentivize seller manipulation.

citations.quote: Why are Dutch auctions so popular in Web3?... A new paper with Andrew Komo and @skominers answers all these questions

items.rationale: Frames restaking as introducing combinatorial, cascading attack risk where a small shock can compound; wants robustness (overcollateralization) made explicit before validators are safely reused.

citations.quote: New paper... offers a framework to reason about the risks of restaking. Key question: Under what conditions can validators be safely reused across multiple services?
if attack costs always exceed attack profits by 10%, then a sudden loss of .1% of the overall stake cannot result in the ultimate loss of more than 1.1%

items.rationale: Proves slashing fails to deliver its intended accountability once an attacker is large enough or when network delays can't be bounded — a caution on overstated PoS security guarantees.

citations.quote: slashing in a proof-of-stake protocol cannot achieve its intended purpose if message delays cannot be bounded a priori

items.rationale: Shows in-protocol defense against long-range attacks requires exotic cryptography (VDFs, ephemeral keys); otherwise PoS chains lean on out-of-protocol social consensus.

citations.quote: fancy primitives like VDFs and ephemeral keys are the *only possible way* for proof-of-stake blockchains to defend against long-range attacks without any out-of-protocol assistance

items.rationale: Not a price/market view — Ethereum is his primary object of study; he frames it as aspiring to a 'best of both worlds' PoS design and notes the merge increased its economic security.
items.age_note: Analytical engagement, not an investment endorsement.

citations.quote: Ethereum is effectively aspiring toward the best of both worlds: the basic guarantees of a longest-chain protocol... plus the extra benefits of a PBFT-style protocol
making precise the common belief that the merge has increased Ethereum's economic security

risk_regime.rationale: Roughgarden's public statements are almost exclusively academic — consensus theory, mechanism design, MEV, restaking, auctions, AMMs, SNARKs. He expresses no market-timing, price-direction, or portfolio risk posture whatsoever.

notes: Corpus (2023-07 to 2024-12) is an academic researcher's feed, not a trader's. 'Sectors' here reflect research enthusiasm/concern, not investment theses; conviction scores measure intellectual intensity, not allocation. Tokens are nearly absent — Bitcoin, Algorand (ephemeral keys), Chia (VDFs), Avalanche, Tendermint appear only as protocol-design examples, never as market calls, so they are not listed as token views. ETH is included only because it is his central analytic subject, flagged as non-market. Most recent themes (Dec 2024): SNARKs/advanced cryptography optimism, LVR/AMM tutorials, and 'mental models for blockchain' explainers aimed at web2 newcomers. No stance reversals detected; views are cumulative and consistent across the window.
